questionHtml: Describe the structure of proteins, including features that are common to all proteins and features that vary.
markschemeHtml:
1. composed of one or more polypeptides / some are single polypeptides others made of 2 or more polypeptide chains / all proteins consist of, at least, one polypeptide chain;
  2. a polypeptide/protein is a chain of amino acids;
  3. (chains of) amino acids linked by peptide bonds;
  4. twenty different amino acids/ amino acids have different R groups/ R groups can be hydrophilic/polar or hydrophobic/non-polar;
  5. primary structure is the sequence/order (and number) of amino acids in the polypeptide;
  6. any sequence/order of amino acids could be linked together hence many possible polypeptides/proteins;
  7. secondary structure is the formation of alpha/ α helices and beta/ β pleated sheets;
  8. secondary structure stabilized by hydrogen bonding;
  9. tertiary structure is the further folding of the polypeptide / tertiary structure is the three-dimensional (3-D) conformation/structure/shape of a protein;
  10. tertiary structure stabilized by interactions / ionic bonds/hydrogen bonds/disulfide bridges between R groups;
  11. fibrous or globular are the two main classes of conformation/three-dimensional/ 3-D structure/shape;
  12. conformation/three-dimensional (3-D) structure determined by amino acid sequence;
  13. quaternary structure if two or more polypeptides are linked to form a (single) protein
    OR
    quaternary structure if a prosthetic group is linked (to form a conjugated protein);

Accept clearly annotated diagrams.

Mpc: accept a clearly annotated diagram of the bond between 2 drawn amino acids.

meta.title: Describe the structure of proteins, including features that are common to all proteins and features that vary.
